names.original: أبو نصر محمد بن محمد الفارابي
names.transliteration: Abū Naṣr Muḥammad ibn Muḥammad al-Fārābī
aliases: al-Mu'allim al-Thani ('the Second Teacher', after Aristotle)

summary: Abu Nasr al-Farabi (c. 872-950 CE), known as 'the Second Teacher' (al-Mu'allim al-Thani, after Aristotle), was a major early philosopher of the falsafa tradition, a logician, and a political philosopher. In works such as Mabadi' Ara' Ahl al-Madina al-Fadila (The Principles of the Opinions of the People of the Virtuous City) he set out a Neoplatonized Aristotelian metaphysics of emanation and a theory of the ideal state and of the relation of philosophy to religion, and he was a decisive influence on Ibn Sina (Avicenna) and later on Maimonides.
description:
Abu Nasr al-Farabi lived c. 872-950, active chiefly at Baghdad, the centre of the Graeco-Arabic learning of the Abbasid period, and later in Aleppo and Damascus. In the falsafa tradition, the current of Hellenistic-derived rational philosophy in the Islamic world, he came to be called 'the Second Teacher' (al-Mu'allim al-Thani): the first being Aristotle, whose logical corpus and its late-antique interpretation he did much to establish in Arabic. He is generally regarded in the scholarship as the first great system-builder of Islamic philosophy and, in Majid Fakhry's phrase, the founder of Islamic Neoplatonism, for combining an Aristotelian logic and cosmology with a Neoplatonic metaphysics of emanation in which all things proceed from a First (al-Awwal, the First Cause) through a hierarchy of intellects.

Al-Farabi was above all a logician; Deborah Black and others place his theory of knowledge largely within his logic, and he wrote extensively on Aristotle's Organon, including the Rhetoric and Poetics, and on the demonstrative certitude (yaqin) that distinguishes philosophy from lesser forms of assent. He also composed an influential essay on the harmony of the two sages, arguing that the apparent disagreements of Plato and Aristotle can be reconciled, and works surveying the philosophy of Plato and of Aristotle and 'the attainment of happiness' (tahsil al-sa'ada) as the end of the philosophical life.

His best-known work is Mabadi' Ara' Ahl al-Madina al-Fadila (The Principles of the Opinions of the People of the Virtuous City, often 'the Virtuous City' or 'the Perfect State'), which sets a metaphysics of the First and the emanated cosmos alongside a political philosophy of the ideal city. In it the virtuous city is ordered, on the analogy of the cosmos and of the healthy body, under a single ruler; its supreme head, in al-Farabi's account, unites the perfections of the philosopher, the lawgiver, and the prophet, one whose intellect is joined to the Active Intellect and who can both grasp the intelligibles and represent them in images to the citizens.

Al-Farabi's account of the relation of philosophy and religion is among his most discussed and most sensitive positions, and it is given here strictly as his attributed philosophical view. On the reading set out by Fakhry and by Peter Adamson, al-Farabi held that philosophy attains truth in demonstrative form through the intellect, while religion (milla) presents the same truths to the multitude in the form of symbols, images, and persuasive speech suited to those who cannot follow demonstration; on this view revealed religion is, for al-Farabi, a symbolic representation (a 'likeness') of philosophical truth, and the prophet is the one whose imaginative faculty renders the intelligibles as images and laws for the community. This is recorded as al-Farabi's own philosophical position within falsafa, not as the editor's account of revelation or prophecy, and not as the view of Islamic theology (kalam) or of the tradition generally, which are treated in their own entries.

His influence was far-reaching. Ibn Sina (Avicenna) reports having understood Aristotle's Metaphysics only on reading al-Farabi's treatise on its aims, and the Avicennan metaphysics of the Necessary Existent and the emanated intellects builds on al-Farabi's foundation; through the falsafa tradition his political philosophy and his theory of prophecy reached later thinkers, and Maimonides, in a much-cited letter, singled out al-Farabi's logical works as of the first rank and drew on his account of prophecy and of the philosopher-lawgiver in the Guide of the Perplexed. These lines of influence are stated here as matters of intellectual history.
editorial_notes: This entry presents al-Farabi on Islam's terms as a philosopher of the falsafa tradition, described in that tradition's own terms before any comparison (neutrality #6), and files him under islam as a figure of Islamic intellectual history. THE SENSITIVE POINT: al-Farabi's account of revealed religion as the symbolic or figurative representation of philosophical truth for the multitude (with the prophet as the one who images the intelligibles) is given strictly as HIS attributed philosophical position within falsafa, per neutrality #1 and #2, and NOT as the editor's view of revelation or prophecy, nor as the teaching of kalam or of the tradition at large; the entry marks it explicitly as his view and does not adjudicate it against the theological schools, which have their own entries. His influence on Ibn Sina and on Maimonides is stated factually as intellectual history, not as endorsement (neutrality #1). Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-#9: (a) modern academic scholarship, Majid Fakhry, Al-Farabi, Founder of Islamic Neoplatonism (Oneworld, 2002), and Deborah L. Black's peer-reviewed article on al-Farabi's epistemology (Arabic Sciences and Philosophy, 2006); (b) al-Farabi's own primary works in scholarly editions and translations, the Virtuous City in Richard Walzer's critical edition and translation (Clarendon, 1985) and the Attainment of Happiness / Philosophy of Plato and Aristotle in Muhsin Mahdi's translation (Cornell, rev. 2001); (c) Peter Adamson, Philosophy in the Islamic World (Oxford, 2016), whose chapters 'The Second Master: al-Farabi' and 'State of Mind: al-Farabi on Religion and Politics' treat exactly the religion-and-philosophy theme. No encyclopedias (no Encyclopaedia of Islam, no Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. CONFIDENCE / HONESTY: all sources are rated MEDIUM. Per the brief, al-Farabi's works survive in proprietary modern critical editions and translations (Walzer, Mahdi), so they were verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher, WorldCat, and peer-reviewed journal-review records but were NOT read line-by-line in an open text; no HIGH rating is claimed, and none is invented. Fakhry, Black, and Adamson are likewise verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher pages, the Cambridge/Liverpool journal record (Black, Arabic Sciences and Philosophy 16.1, 2006, 11-46), and a scholarly review (The Medieval Review) but not read line-by-line. Source-diversity gap (flagged honestly per neutrality #8): all five sources are Western-published academic works or Western-edited translations; the tradition's own classical Muslim voice on al-Farabi (a heresiographical or biographical placement within falsafa, or Ibn Sina's own report of reading him cited to a primary edition) is not yet represented, and would strengthen the entry. Relation targets: ibn-sina, aristotle (philosophy), maimonides (judaism), and the falsafa concept exist; al-kindi and plato are dangling forward references (al-kindi to a not-yet-created islam figure; plato exists under philosophy).

lifespan.note: c. 872-950 CE (c. 259-339 AH). The birth year is approximate and traditionally placed c. 870-872, in the region of Farab (in Transoxania) or, on some reports, Faryab; he was active mainly at Baghdad and later at Aleppo and Damascus, where he died in 950/951. Dates and birthplace are approximate.

relations.note: A founding figure of the falsafa tradition and, in Fakhry's characterization, the founder of Islamic Neoplatonism, combining Aristotelian logic and cosmology with a Neoplatonic metaphysics of emanation; the falsafa concept entry exists.

Ibn Sina (Avicenna) reports having grasped Aristotle's Metaphysics only after reading al-Farabi's treatise on its aims, and the Avicennan metaphysics of the emanated intellects builds on al-Farabi's foundation; the ibn-sina entry exists. Stated as intellectual history.

Maimonides praised al-Farabi's logical works in a much-cited letter and drew on his account of prophecy and of the philosopher-lawgiver in the Guide of the Perplexed; the maimonides entry exists (judaism). Stated as intellectual history, not doctrinal agreement.

Al-Farabi was called 'the Second Teacher' after Aristotle ('the First Teacher') and established much of the Aristotelian logical corpus in Arabic, commenting on the Organon; the aristotle entry exists (philosophy).
Al-Farabi drew on Plato's political philosophy for his account of the virtuous city and its philosopher-ruler and argued for the harmony of Plato and Aristotle; the plato entry exists (philosophy).

Al-Kindi, the earliest of the falasifa, preceded al-Farabi in the falsafa tradition at Baghdad; the two are the first figures of Islamic philosophy in the standard accounts. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created islam figure entity.

sources.citation: Fakhry, Majid. Al-Farabi, Founder of Islamic Neoplatonism: His Life, Works and Influence. Great Islamic Thinkers. Oxford: Oneworld, 2002. The standard single-volume academic introduction to al-Farabi's life, works, and influence, treating his logic, his Neoplatonized Aristotelian metaphysics of emanation, his political philosophy of the virtuous city, and his account of the relation of philosophy and religion; cited as the modern academic lens and for the characterization of al-Farabi as founder of Islamic Neoplatonism. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Oneworld publisher page; review in International Journal of Middle East Studies), not read line-by-line.

Black, Deborah L. 'Knowledge (ʿilm) and Certitude (yaqīn) in al-Fārābī's Epistemology.' Arabic Sciences and Philosophy 16, no. 1 (2006): 11-46. A peer-reviewed study of al-Farabi's theory of knowledge and of demonstrative certitude, situating his epistemology within his logic; cited as the modern academic lens on al-Farabi as logician and epistemologist. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Cambridge / Liverpool University Press journal record), not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary journal article; cited for reference (publisher record).

sources.citation: al-Farabi, Abu Nasr. Al-Farabi on the Perfect State: Abū Naṣr al-Fārābī's Mabādi' Ārā' Ahl al-Madīna al-Fāḍila. A revised text with introduction, translation, and commentary by Richard Walzer. Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1985. Primary source: the critical edition and English translation of al-Farabi's 'Principles of the Opinions of the People of the Virtuous City', the source for his metaphysics of the First and the emanated cosmos and for his political philosophy of the virtuous city and its philosopher-prophet ruler. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Clarendon Press; reviews in the Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society and the Bulletin of SOAS), cited from a proprietary translation and not read line-by-line, so rated medium.
sources.license: Proprietary translation of a classical text; cited for reference (relevant passages not read line-by-line).

sources.citation: Alfarabi (al-Farabi). Philosophy of Plato and Aristotle. Translated with an introduction by Muhsin Mahdi; foreword by Charles E. Butterworth and Thomas L. Pangle. Agora Editions. Ithaca, NY: Cornell University Press, revised edition 2001 (first published 1962/1969). Primary source: comprising the Attainment of Happiness (Tahsil al-Sa'ada), the Philosophy of Plato, and the Philosophy of Aristotle, the source for al-Farabi's account of the attainment of happiness as the end of the philosophical life and of the harmony of Plato and Aristotle. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Cornell University Press page), cited from a proprietary translation and not read line-by-line, so rated medium.
sources.license: Proprietary translation of a classical text; cited for reference (relevant passages not read line-by-line).

sources.citation: Adamson, Peter. Philosophy in the Islamic World. A History of Philosophy Without Any Gaps, Volume 3. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016. An academic history of philosophy in the Islamic world whose chapters 'The Second Master: al-Farabi' and 'State of Mind: al-Farabi on Religion and Politics' treat al-Farabi's logic and metaphysics and, in particular, his account of the relation of philosophy and religion and of prophecy; cited as the contemporary scholarly lens on al-Farabi and specifically on the religion-and-philosophy theme. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Oxford University Press pages; review in The Medieval Review), not read line-by-line.
